text: The great majority of natural and engineered systems consist of interacting entities in intricate patterns of multiple types of links and relationships, over different time periods, and with diverse sets of additional characteristics. Such systems are multi-component, multi-link, and with many layers of structure. To make progress on complex systems, it is necessary to start from traditional network science and develop its generalization to the analysis of multilayer systems, along with the corresponding set of computational tools that analyze the structure and behavior of such systems. The study of such systems has been an active research theme since the 1960s in many different fields, and, in recent years, the study of multilayer networks has become one of the main thrusts in network science. In this paper we briefly review the history of multilayer networks and related work. We then focus on a unifying approach to multilayer networks and define a dictionary of words, which relates various terms that have recently been introduced in the large body of multilayer network work, and we summarize the current state-of-the-art of multilayer networks, focusing on the basics as well as associated models and analysis approaches that are beginning to appear in the literature. We discuss various multi-layer network datasets that have been published, we review the growing number of metrics and analysis tools developed in order to study multilayer network structures and their evolution. We also summarize recent modeling efforts toward the study of multilayer networks focusing on communities, connected components, tensor decomposition and dynamics. We then conclude with final remarks.
